original_abstract: Every national legal system must establish rigorous frameworks to manage threats that jeopardize the state’s stability or very existence. These extraordinary circumstances, defined as states of emergency, shift primary responsibility for public safety to state administration bodies. To ensure effective governance during crises, legislative provisions must clearly delineate the scope of authority and liability for these entities, ensuring that emergency powers are invoked only when standard constitutional measures prove inadequate. This article examines the evolution of emergency legal institutions in the newly independent Polish state, focusing on the frameworks established by the March Constitution of 1921 and the April Constitution of 1935. Additionally, it analyzes subsidiary legal acts from this era that were pivotal in safeguarding national security. By employing dogmatic-legal and historical-legal methodologies, this study explores how specific regulations were justified by the shifting internal and external pressures of the interwar period, illustrating the legal transition from precarious independence to centralized crisis management.
humanized_abstract: All national law systems must ensure appropriate measures to counter threats to the security of the state in a situation when the very existence of the state is at risk. Such a situation is known as an emergency or state of emergency. In this situation, the state administration bodies assume the main responsibility for maintaining public safety, while the legislation must establish the scope of their authority and liability in this respect, ensuring that the exercising emergency powers is only possible when the constitutional means provided for under normal circumstances are ineffective. The article reflects the regulation of emergency law institutions in the newly established Polish state in the period between the March Constitution of 1921 and the April Constitution of 1935, as well as the sublegislative acts adopted in this period to protect national security. The research is carried out by means of dogmatic-legal and historical-legal methods. It explains how individual regulations were established and what the various challenges – internal and external – faced by Poland in the interwar period looked like, illustrating the transition of Poland from a state on the brink of collapse to a state able to provide order in the case of a crisis.
